evidence: Docstring asks 'Is 10 a solitary number?', the smallest integer whose solitary status is unresolved, per the referenced Solitary number Wikipedia article.
Solitary is defined via the Friendly predicate ('every friend of n is equal to n'), i.e. equality of abundancy index.
The statement asks whether any abundancy club {m | Friendly m n} is infinite, directly about friendly numbers.
The file's reference is the 'Solitary number#Large clubs' Wikipedia section, where the infinite-club question is posed.
Wichmann's conjecture states every optimal sparse ruler with more than 13 segments is a Wichmann ruler; the file references the Sparse ruler Wikipedia article.
The IsComplete hypothesis is glossed 'a ruler is *complete* (a perfect ruler) if its marks form a difference basis'.
Asks for the smallest square containing 11 unit squares, citing 'Wikipedia Square_packing#In_a_square'.
Asks for the smallest square containing 17 unit squares, citing 'Wikipedia Square_packing#In_a_square'.
Asks for the smallest circle containing 3 unit squares, citing 'Wikipedia Square_packing#In_a_circle' (a section of the same Square packing article).
Asks for the smallest square containing 21 unit circles; the file header references the 'packing of circles in a square' Wikipedia article.
Asks for the smallest circle containing 15 unit circles; the file header references the 'packing of circles in a circle' Wikipedia article.
Asks to construct an S(t,k,n) Steiner system with t > 5; no Steiner system with t > 5 is explicitly known, per the referenced Steiner system article and Epoch AI problem.
States existence of the Steiner system S(5,6,12), the small Witt design, one of only two known Steiner systems with t = 5.
States existence of the Steiner system S(5,8,24), the large Witt design constructed by Witt (1938).
The docstring names S(5,8,24) 'the large Witt design'; the Wikidata item Witt design carries the alias 'S(5, 8, 24)'.
States there are infinitely many Steiner systems with t = 4, via Keevash's 2014 existence theorem.
States there are infinitely many Steiner systems with t = 5, settled nonconstructively by Keevash (2014).

The decl states the sums-of-three-cubes problem exactly as the Wikidata item describes it: characterizing the representable integers as those not 4 or 5 mod 9, per the referenced Sums of three cubes article.
Ryley's 1825 theorem that every rational is a sum of three rational cubes, a solved case within the sums-of-three-cubes problem the file is about.
The (m,k)-perfect condition sigma^m(n) = kn generalizes superperfect numbers; the file references the 'Superperfect number#Generalizations' Wikipedia section.
Gottschalk's conjecture (1973) states every group is surjunctive; the file references the Surjunctive group Wikipedia article, and no Wikidata item for the conjecture itself was found.
Asks whether a taxicab number exists for k=5, m=2, n=2; the file references the Taxicab number Wikipedia article.
The k=5 (fifth powers) case is the generalized taxicab problem; the file cites the Generalized taxicab number Wikipedia article, where the sum-of-two-fifth-powers question is posed as open.
Asks whether a taxicab number exists for k=5, m=2 and any n >= 2, per the referenced Taxicab number article.
No integer is known expressible as a sum of two fifth powers in more than one way — the open question from the cited Generalized taxicab number article.
States the Toronto problem — every T2 Toronto space is discrete — from the referenced Toronto space Wikipedia article and W.R. Brian's note.
